jokes.explanation: The interrupted sentence is a great comedic structure — the audience can complete 'You're just gonna be [insufferable/a nightmare/unbearable]' and Jerry's self-censorship under Helen's 'Excuse me?' is perfectly in character. The beat captures the parent-child dynamic where the adult child can't quite say the honest thing.

jokes.text: And he says that according to the bylaws of the condo constitution... they need six votes to throw you out for unruly behavior. Not five.
jokes.setup: Morty is facing ejection from the condo association for the testimonial brawl
jokes.punchline: The existence of specific constitutional bylaws governing the number of votes needed for 'unruly behavior' ejection — and the crucial distinction between five and six — applied to a condo association
jokes.explanation: The comedy of a condo association that has a fully elaborated constitutional framework for ejecting members for 'unruly behavior' — with specific vote counts — is excellent. The 'not five' precision echoes the $19.45 precision earlier. The gravity with which this constitutional matter is treated mirrors the whole episode's theme of inflated stakes.

jokes.text: Dr. Chernov is the one you'll have to suck up to.
jokes.setup: The vote count and strategy for Morty's political survival is being discussed
jokes.punchline: 'Dr. Chernov is the one you'll have to suck up to' — delivered as pragmatic political advice by Helen, completely matter-of-fact
jokes.explanation: 'Suck up to' as the official political strategy recommended by the president's wife is funny for its directness. Helen delivers this with the assurance of a seasoned political operative, which is even funnier given the context of condo politics. Good economy.

jokes.text: Oh, my back. That bar. Who the hell could sleep on that thing? / I was very comfortable.
jokes.setup: Elaine is suffering from the sofa bed injury
jokes.punchline: Helen's completely sincere 'I was very comfortable' — she apparently loves the sofa bed that destroyed Elaine's back
jokes.explanation: The callback to the sofa bed saga with a perfect punchline: Helen is comfortable on the thing that injured Elaine. This makes Helen either superhuman or deeply perverse. The deadpan sincerity of 'I was very comfortable' is the right note.

jokes.text: Who are you? / I'm a chiropractor.
jokes.setup: A stranger is in the room; Morty hasn't been introduced
jokes.punchline: The chiropractor's calm professional introduction to someone who clearly wasn't expecting him
jokes.explanation: A quick transitional beat. Morty's bewilderment at finding a stranger in his home who turns out to be a chiropractor is mildly funny. The joke is in the casualness of the reveal. Not one of the episode's stronger moments.

jokes.text: What, are you kidding me? Five more days? / Well, today's almost over, and weekdays always go by fast. / And Friday, we're leaving, so it's like two days, really. / It's like a cup of coffee. / It'll go by like that.
jokes.setup: Five more days of Florida has been established; both are trying to cope
jokes.punchline: The same fraudulent time-compression logic from Joke 35 now applied to five days — which somehow becomes 'a cup of coffee'
jokes.explanation: The callback to the time-compression routine is perfectly executed. The original reduced a day to 20 minutes; now five days is reduced to 'a cup of coffee.' The escalation of the self-deception — applied to a much longer duration — makes the absurdism more extreme. 'It's like a cup of coffee' is the peak: a duration so brief as to be barely perceptible. 'It'll go by like that' is the shared mantra they've developed. Strong structure for a callback: it's not just a repeat, it's an escalation.

jokes.text: If they ever decide to land men on the sun... I think these old retired guys will be the only ones that will be able to handle it. They'll sit there on the redwood benches, washcloth on their head, going: 'I'm trying to get a sweat going.'
jokes.setup: Jerry observes that Florida retirees love impossible heat
jokes.punchline: Old men on the surface of the sun trying to get a sweat going — the ultimate absurdist extension of the heat/temperature theme
jokes.explanation: The closing stand-up bit is a perfect payoff to the episode's temperature running gag. The image of elderly retirees on the surface of the sun with washcloths trying to break a sweat is inspired absurdism. The specificity — 'redwood benches,' 'washcloth on their head' — grounds the absurd image in the real-world details of the Florida retirement community we've just visited. 'I'm trying to get a sweat going' is a perfect closing line: the attitude is both recognizable and completely insane. This earns high craft scores because it takes the episode's thematic thread and lands it with genuine imagination.

tmdb_overview: Elaine regrets accompanying Jerry on a visit to his parents, where local gossip includes how Jerry acquired a pen that writes upside down.
insight: Escalation-driven cringe builds through 74 jokes, anchored by George's signature social dysfunction.
